Name: geometric_precision_routing
Title: Geometric Precision Routing: Training-Free Mixed-Precision Quantization via Curvature-Aware Subspace Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging curvature-aware precision routing and geometric subspace diagnostics, we can achieve efficient mixed-precision quantization without the need for training, while maintaining formalized stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods often rely on training-based search or heuristic proxies, which lack formal guarantees and understanding of the underlying geometric properties. Recent works like EMQ and AMP-ViT have made strides in training-free approaches, but they do not fully exploit the geometric structure of the model's curvature and subspace diagnostics.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) is crucial for deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained devices. However, existing methods often rely on training-based search or heuristic proxies, which lack formal guarantees and understanding of the underlying geometric properties. This paper introduces Geometric Precision Routing (GPR), a novel framework that leverages curvature-aware precision routing and geometric subspace diagnostics to achieve efficient MPQ without the need for training. GPR employs deterministic spectral estimators and pre-execution diagnostic loops to formally isolate algorithmic pathologies from infrastructural noise, enabling a training-free routing protocol that replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds. We validate GPR on various architectures and datasets, demonstrating superior performance in terms of accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate GPR on Vision Transformers (ViTs) using ImageNet dataset.
Compare GPR with state-of-the-art methods (EMQ, AMP-ViT) on ResNet and MobileNet families.
Assess the impact of curvature-aware precision routing on model stability using formalized stability radii.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contribution of geometric subspace diagnostics.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of GPR may vary across different architectures and datasets.
The computational overhead of pre-execution diagnostic loops needs to be carefully managed.
The formalized stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds may not fully capture all aspects of model performance.
